publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 126-128. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Finding no symptoms of either breakfast (no cloth being laid), or of human beings, I have sat down to write to you, in my shawl & boa in their very cold Sitting Room.

I was down at nine, having been told there would then be breakfast. We all sat up reading & talking philosophy till one o'clock last night. I suppose that is the secret of the dawdle this morning. My head is very muzzy this morning, from that cause I think; & I shall take the liberty this evening of calling
my hosts to a recollection of Time. The droll thing was we were discussing the metaphysics of Time & Space, & in so doing we forgot real Time & Space.

We got here at 1/2 past three!-- Our horses absolutely flew. We were at Williton under two hours. I never saw the poor things go so beautifully. Crosse was astonished. They baited a full hour at Williton, & had a 10 minutes rest & a little water again at the foot of the great ridge we crossed, which is indeed a tremendous ascent, of I think a mile long-- more or less steep.
But the beasts made nothing of it, & came in quite fresh & lively. They seemed pleased when the Bird fluttered round
Our journey was agreeable. I was so admirably clad that I did not feel the cold.

In the course of it, I was able to give Crosse a complete outline & vista of all my scientific plans & ideas. He seems to think there is much merit in them, & remarked, "I see no mere enthu= "siasm in all this. It is all "so quietly reasoned, so soundly "based".--

I find my visit here is of more importance to me than I anticipated. The eldest son John is giving me information as to the scientific doings in Germany which it is of the utmost importance I should be

in possession of; & he will undertake, if I choose, to be my organ to any extent he can, in procuring me every means of keeping au courant as to German mathematics & Natural Philosophy. He is a most extensively read, & also a very clever young man. I can get from & by means of him, what I could from no one else. He is to be 6 months at Berlin next year, & he will occupy himself in catering for me. He knows much not only about German metaphysics & theology, but about their physiology, &c.
It will be a great thing for my views to have so many labourers in the field for me.-- John Crosse says that in Germany books of dry

science sell even better than light works;-- that for instance such a one as Wilkinson's would pay richly, had it been written in German. This is a hint which I shall endeavour to get more information upon; for my own future purposes. I believe that many of my subjects would be read to an extent, in that country, which they never would here, (besides paying well).--
But all this I shall by degrees take measures for obtaining authentic data for judging of.
It would be a proud thing to write German scientific treatises. I know very nearly enough of the language.--
It is bitterly cold, which don't suit me.
